data.title: Global Commodities Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Commodities. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

1212 ET - The USDA reported a new round of flash sales of U.S. corn exports, with two sales reported to unknown destinations for delivery in the 2025/26 marketing year. A total of 352,160 metric tons of corn were sold, according to the USDA. Export demand is still a major driver for CBOT grain futures, but it doesn't seem to be pushing futures into positive territory today. "The market continues to be weighed down by the large U.S. crop on the way," says John Stewart and Associates in a note. Corn is down 0.7%, and wheat is off 1.2%. Soybeans are up 0.3%. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

1129 ET - CBOT soybean futures are up 0.3% in morning trading, slightly reversing the losing streak seen in soybeans across the past five trading sessions, and eight out of the past nine trading days. Soybeans fell under the $10/bushel mark and shed nearly 10% of their value in that time. Fund traders have taken an increasingly-negative view on the prospects for futures, Matt Zeller of StoneX says in a note. "Soybeans have taken the brunt of the funds' wrath heading into the key month of August, starting to build up a moderate overall net short according to daily fund estimates." The CFTC will update its outlook for the Commitment of Traders report this afternoon, with short positions in soybeans built. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

1113 ET - Gold futures rise after U.S. economic data came in softer than expected. Futures are up 1.6% at $3,402.30 a troy ounce. Nonfarm Payroll data showed just 73,000 jobs were added in July, below analyst expectations of 100,000. Even more suprising, revisions to May and June's data revised jobs down by a huge 258,000, ING analysts say in a note. This highlights that the jobs market has lost momentum even earlier than thought and will intensify pressure from President Trump for the Federal Reserve to loosen monetary policy, ING says. With tariffs set to eat into household spending and corporate profits, the case for cutting interest rates has been significantly strengthened, ING says. Lower interest rates typically benefit non-interest bearing bullion. (joseph.hoppe@wsj.com)

1102 ET — Lean hog futures on the CME are down 0.7% in morning trade, as traders and analysts mull what the flood of new rules mean for U.S. pork. Fortunately, the largest buyers of U.S. pork--Mexico and Canada--are still covered under the USMCA, meaning that their export sales aren't being limited by tariffs. "For pork, nothing specific...pork remains in the free-trade agreement bucket with both Mexico and Canada which remains in effect," says StoneX in a note. Live cattle futures are down 0.2% in morning trade. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

1053 ET - Overall, Imperial Oil had a good quarter, reckons Raymond James's Michael Barth. The analyst says the energy company managed a modest 4% beat on funds from operations over consensus estimates on what largely appears to be slightly stronger production. Still, given that Imperial is one of the best performing large-cap Canadian energy names, Barth is skeptical the latest quarter will be enough to drive meaningful outperformance against its peer group from here. Shares are down 1% at C$114.34. (robb.stewart@wsj.com)

1005 ET - U.S. crops managed to make it out of June and July mostly unscathed, with the U.S. Drought Monitor reporting shrinking drought coverage of farmland across the country this week. Of Corn Belt states, only North Dakota and Nebraska are seeing any large areas affected by dryness, while for the rest of the Corn Belt dryness its practically nonexistent--making a record-sized crops forecast by the USDA this year more likely. "A year ago heat in the second half of August and rapid net soil moisture loss stole from potential from final yields," says Daniel Flynn of Price Futures Group in a note. CBOT corn falls 0.5%, soybeans slide 0.2%, and wheat is down 0.9%. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

0935 ET - U.S. natural gas futures are slightly lower after yesterday's recovery from a dip below $3. Prices could find support from August weather forecasts showing more heat for the 6-10 and 11-15 day periods, Tradition Energy's Gary Cunningham says in a note. Possible record temperatures next week in the southwest "could push electricity demands in southern California to levels requiring extra dispatch of gas peakers," he adds. "We expect the September contract to be range-bound between $3 and $3.20 with some resistance at $3.15." September gas is off 0.6% at $3.088/mmBtu. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

0931 ET - The record loss recorded by U.S. copper futures Thursday, with the most-active contract plummeting 22% after President Trump raised tariffs on imported copper, is spurring some bargain bin buying. That's pushing the U.S. futures contract up 1.5% to $4.42 a pound. "Copper is higher, attempting to recover from some of yesterday's steep losses that occurred in response to the imposition of 50% tariffs on certain forms," says Peter Cardillo of Spartan Capital Securities in a note. Cardillo adds that for macro markets and commodities in general, the arrival of the Aug. 1 deadline and weaker hiring in July are pressuring prices. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

0927 ET - Enbridge may have affirmed its Ebitda guidance for 2025 where investors may have wanted to see a lift given a strong 1H performance, but its results overall were solid, Jefferies' Sam Burwell says. The energy company's 2Q Ebitda beat the consensus forecast by 2.5%, driven by its U.S. gas transmission business, and it ended the quarter with debt leverage of 4.7 times, the analyst notes. Liquids earnings were in line with what Burwell anticipated, the renewables business was slightly below his forecast, but has transmission offered a solid beat and gas distribution was modestly ahead of his forecast. Jefferies has a one-year forward target of C$72 on the shares, which were last at C$62.75. (robb.stewart@wsj.com)

0917 ET - Tariffs levied on countries like Brazil exempted a number of goods, including industrial metals and energy. But tariffs notably have not contained any exemptions for soft commodities like cocoa and coffee--goods that can't be grown in the U.S. due to a lack of the proper climate. That may soon change, says Secretary of Commerce Howard Lutnick. In an appearance on CNBC this week, Lutnick said President Trump would consider zero tariffs on natural resources that can't be produced domestically. No specifics were offered, but Lutnick says this could happen if individual deals are reached with producing countries like Brazil and the Ivory Coast. Coffee on the ICE is down 0.2%, and ICE cocoa is down 0.9%. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

0912 ET - Oil futures flit between small gains and losses as the U.S. tariff deadline passes, with tariffs due to kick in on Aug. 7, fueling concerns about demand. "Values are being supported by the need to maintain risk premium for possibly another week on the chance that secondary tariffs may be placed on importers of Russian oil," Ritterbusch says in a note. "Oil price downside in response to expected weakening in economic and oil demand growth paths is being limited mainly to the product markets." WTI is up 0.1% at $69.30 a barrel, and Brent is down 0.1% at $71.65 as October moves to the front of the curve. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

0906 ET - The July jobs report showing fewer new jobs added than economists expected sends gold futures up, pushing the most-active contract 1.3% higher after starting the day virtually flat. The government also said revisions for May and June were larger than normal. The report is being taken as a sign the labor market may be weaker than previously believed. The data is likely to add more pressure for Fed to cut rates in September. Fed Chair Powell's comments after leaving interest rates unchanged Wednesday had been keeping a lid on gold, with Powell not willing to signal if a cut was coming next month. "Powell thus gave the impression that everything was still open," says Commerzbank in a note. The dollar has turned weaker, with the WSJ index down 0.8%. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

data.title: Russia could face gasoline shortages despite export ban, sources say
data.description:
Russia could face gasoline shortages in August despite an export ban, as low domestic stocks, peak seasonal demand and repair work at domestic refineries affect the market, trade sources said on Friday.

The ban, imposed on Monday and intended to last until August 31, is aimed at keeping the Russian market stabilised and avoiding socially sensitive motor fuel price increases.

Yet traders say it will likely not be enough to balance the market, as gasoline export volumes are far smaller than domestic consumption and will do little to satisfy demand even if diverted to the local market.

They expect oil companies to be forced by state regulators to sell more refinery products domestically and delay the planned plant maintenance.

Russia’s Energy Ministry didn’t immediately reply to a request for comment sent after business hours on Friday.

This year private retail networks have not created sufficient fuel reserves to meet high demand in the summer, due to a spike in interest rates to 20% which made borrowing money from banks to buy fuel in advance too expensive, according to market participants.

"Currently, gasoline production is at a normal level for the summer. The sales are also within expectations. But private traders lack stocks," a source with a large oil company said.

Frequent flight delays at Russian airports have also resulted in higher gasoline consumption, as travellers switch to their cars, sources at gasoline retailers said.

The shortage of gasoline will likely persist through September, market participants and industry experts believe, though prices may decline in October, when local refineries will complete repairs, while demand will come off its seasonal peaks.
